recommendation: Simulation is the correct orchestration method for Trellix Insights because the product is a cloud-only SaaS portal with no self-hosted deployment option, no per-learner provisioning API, and OTP-based authentication that blocks automated account creation at scale. Trellix's own 'Take a Tour' interactive demo confirms this is how the product is explored without a live environment — Skillable Simulation mirrors this approach with the addition of guided scoring checkpoints and AI Vision validation. Build simulation assets from Trellix's published product documentation, threat intelligence reports, and the existing interactive tour as source material.

Score Trellix Insights labs via Simulation guided interaction checkpoints at each analytical decision point — correct threat actor identified, correct TTPs mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, correct vulnerability priority assigned. AI Vision provides a secondary validation layer for dashboard navigation tasks. MCQ knowledge checks supplement for conceptual understanding of threat intelligence frameworks. This combination covers the full analytical workflow without requiring live API access.

Trellix Insights alone is a simulation candidate with constrained ACV — the real lab opportunity is the broader Trellix Security Platform. Trellix Endpoint Security, ePolicy Orchestrator (ePO), Helix (SIEM/SOAR), and Network Detection and Response all have installable components that run on Hyper-V VMs with rich CLI and API surfaces. A multi-product lab program covering the full platform would score significantly higher on Product Labability, deliver far more training value, and generate substantially higher consumption. Recommend scoping a platform-level engagement rather than an Insights-only pilot.

The key technical question that unblocks a better lab path: 'Does Trellix Insights have a sandbox API, developer tenant, or demo environment that allows programmatic provisioning of isolated instances for training purposes?' Direct this to the Trellix Education Services team and the Insights product team. A verbatim question your champion can send: 'We're evaluating hands-on lab delivery for Trellix Insights training. Does the product have a sandbox or developer environment API that would allow us to provision isolated instances per learner, or is simulation the only viable path?' If a sandbox API exists, the provisioning score improves significantly and real-environment labs become viable.

Simulation-based Insights labs serve three immediate program types: (1) Customer onboarding — new Insights customers need guided practice before they can use the product independently; (2) SOC analyst skill development — existing customers whose analysts underutilize the platform; (3) Internal SE enablement — Trellix sales engineers who need to demonstrate Insights workflows in customer demos. The Commvault expansion arc (SE enablement → customer training → partner training → certification) is the right trajectory to propose, starting with SE enablement as the lowest-friction entry point.

recommendation: Deploy the Trellix Security Platform on Hyper-V using a multi-VM topology: one Windows Server VM running ePO as the management console, two Windows endpoint VMs as managed agents, and optionally a network sensor VM for NDR labs. This mirrors the Tanium pattern — agent-based cybersecurity platform, Hyper-V fabric, Pre-Instancing required to absorb the 30+ minute ePO setup time. The VM image IS the lab; snapshot revert handles teardown automatically. This is the correct path because Trellix is fundamentally installable enterprise software with no cloud dependency for core functionality, and Hyper-V gives full control over the isolated network topology needed for realistic attack simulation.

Primary scoring via ePO REST API and PowerShell: validate agent deployment status, policy application, DLP rule triggers, and EDR detection events programmatically. For simulated attack scenarios, score by verifying that the correct alert was generated and the correct containment action was taken — both queryable via API. Peripheral products (email security, mobile) may require AI Vision fallback for GUI-state validation. A hybrid scoring approach covers the full platform curriculum without gaps.

Trellix's existing Education Services catalog — with explicit hands-on lab exercises across product training and security training tracks — signals a training organization ready to scale. The natural program arc mirrors Commvault: start with internal SE enablement (presales engineers who demo the platform), expand to customer onboarding labs, then partner/ATP enablement, then add PBT scoring to the existing certification program. The certification program is a direct PBT entry vector — Trellix already has the credentialing infrastructure; Skillable adds the performance-based assessment layer.

The critical unresolved question is licensing: does Trellix offer an NFR, evaluation, or lab-scale license tier that allows training delivery without per-learner commercial license costs? The team to ask is Education Services leadership or the product licensing team. A verbatim question your champion can send: 'We're evaluating hands-on lab delivery for Trellix training programs. Do you have an NFR or training-use license tier for ePO and endpoint agents that covers lab environments at scale — for example, 500-5,000 lab sessions per year — without triggering full commercial licensing costs?' This question unblocks the build timeline and cost model simultaneously.

No incumbent lab platform was identified in research — Trellix is a greenfield opportunity. Skillable's multi-VM private networking capability is decisive for cybersecurity labs: realistic attack simulation requires isolated network segments where malware can execute safely, lateral movement can be observed, and network traffic can be monitored. CloudShare cannot support this topology; Instruqt cannot go deep into Windows Server and multi-component enterprise security stacks. Skillable is the only platform that can deliver the full Trellix Security Platform lab experience at the fidelity the curriculum requires.

Provisioning time exceeds 30 minutes for full ePO setup including database initialization, requiring Pre-Instancing. No clearly documented NFR/evaluation license tier for lab-scale deployment — vendor engagement needed before authoring begins.

**Evaluation Licensing | Risk:** No publicly documented NFR or developer license tier found for Trellix — evaluation licenses require direct vendor engagement, adding friction to lab authoring timelines and creating cost uncertainty at lab scale.

key_technical_questions.heading: Key Technical Questions
bullets: Simulation data surface — Director of Training or Director of Customer Education, Training/Enablement team. SecondSight is a SaaS-only threat-intelligence overlay; for a hands-on lab we'd likely simulate its alert triage and investigation workflows rather than provision live instances. Does SecondSight expose a REST or GraphQL API that returns sample detection events, IOC enrichment data, or investigation timelines we could replay against a sandboxed dataset, or would we need to work from exported JSON/STIX bundles to seed a simulation environment?

NFR / sandbox licensing — VP of Education Services or VP of Customer Enablement, Education Services. To build learner-facing labs at scale we need either a dedicated training tenant or per-session API keys that don't count against production entitlements. Does Trellix offer an NFR or education-tier license for SecondSight that allows spinning up isolated, read-only analyst sessions with pre-loaded threat data, and if so, what is the provisioning lead time and seat/session cap?

Identity & multi-tenancy model — Principal Engineer or Solutions Architect, SecondSight Product/Engineering team. When a lab spins up a learner session, we need to authenticate that user without touching a customer's production IdP. Does SecondSight support SCIM or just-in-time SAML/OIDC user provisioning into a segregated tenant, and can those ephemeral user accounts be programmatically deleted via API at lab teardown?

conversation_starters.heading: Conversation Starters
bullets: Trellix's threat detection and response workflows span five different security domains—endpoint, data, network, email, and cloud—so your teams need to practice orchestrating across all of them before they're confident responding to a real incident.

Since SecondSight uses AI to surface threats, your analysts need hands-on time learning how to interpret and act on those AI-generated alerts without the pressure of a live environment—that's where simulation training really pays off.

account_intelligence.heading: Account Intelligence
bullets: Trellix delivers AI-powered security platform across 5+ domains (endpoint, data, network, email, cloud) — likely requires hands-on lab training for customers to operationalize threat detection and response capabilities
SaaS-only deployment model suggests customer enablement is critical to adoption; VP of Education Services/Customer Enablement is a key stakeholder for training infrastructure decisions
